title: Adversarial Samples Defense Strategy Based on Service Orchestration

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

MANIFOLD DISTANCE JUDGE, AN ADVERSARIAL SAMPLES DEFENSE STRATEGY BASED ON SERVICE ORCHESTRATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Deep neural networks (DNNs) are playing an increasingly significant role in the modern world. However, they are weak to adversarial examples that are generated by adding specially crafted perturbations. Most defenses against adversarial examples focused on refining the DNN models, which often sacrifice the performance and computational cost of models on benign samples. In this paper, we propose a manifold distance detection method to distinguish between legitimate samples and adversarial samples by measuring the different distances on the manifold. The manifold distance detection method neither modifies the protected models nor requires knowledge of the process for generating adversarial samples. Inspired by the effectiveness of the manifold distance detection, we demonstrated a well-designed orchestrated defense strategy, named Manifold Distance Judge (MDJ), which selects the best image processing method that will effectively expand the manifold distance between legitimate and adversarial samples, and thus, enhances the performance of the following manifold distance detection method. Tests on the ImageNet dataset, the MDJ is effective against the most adversarial samples under whitebox, graybox, and blackbox attack scenarios. We show empirically that the orchestration strategy MDJ is significantly better than Feature Squeezing on the recall rate. Meanwhile, MDJ achieves high detection rates against CW attack and DI-FGSM attack.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) is the main research direction in artificial intelligence in recent years. Research and applications have shown that deep learning has replaced previous related technologies, and extraordinary breakthroughs have been made in image recognition, security-sensitive applications, and other fields(Kaiming He & Sun, 2016). However, DNNs are weak to adversarial attacks(Christian Szegedy & Fergus, 2014). Attackers can deceive the DNNs model by manipulating graph structure or node features, which results in graph counter disturbances, and limits their application in safety-critical systems. We can divide the attack into two different attack methods based on the attack principles. One is the gradient attack based on the known optimization model, and another one is based on the transfer attack model.

Recent researches have shown that we can use the Manifold Hypothesis theory to explain deep learning models from the mapping of data in high-dimensional space and low-dimensional space(Fefferman et al., 2016)The Manifold Hypothesis states that real-world high-dimensional data lie on low-dimensional manifolds embedded within the high-dimensional space(Aamari, 2019). This Hypothesis is important in DNN. Manifold hypothesis can explain the learning and detection process of deep learning models in image classification, as well as the principle of attacking against attacks(Fefferman & Narayanan, 2019). The Manifold Hypothesis explains why machine learning techniques are able to find useful features and produce accurate predictions from datasets that have a potentially large number of dimensions. From another perspective, the manifold hypothesis also explains why the adversarial attack can attack successfully and achieve the effect of confusing the deep learning model.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

We can use the theory of manifold hypothesis to explain the three adversarial defense methods. Current defenses against adversarial examples follow three approaches: (1) Training the target classifier with adversarial examples, called adversarial training(Christian Szegedy & Fergus, 2014; Ian J. Goodfellow & Szegedy, 2015); (2) Making target classifiers hard to attack by blocking gradient pathway(Papernot et al., 2016b), e.g., defensive distillation and (3) training a classifier to distinguish between normal and adversarial examples(Fischer et al., 2017), e.g., Preprocessing.

In the analysis of defense methods using the manifold hypothesis, we can prove that in the deep learning model, the accuracy and generalization of the model cannot be taken into account at the same time. That is, when we improve the performance of one aspect, we will sacrifice another one. It also illustrates the necessity of building an adversarial sample defense system. Finally, we can also conclude that it is inevitable to produce adversarial samples. On the other hand, in systematic adversarial sample detection methods, there are also methods such as adversarial training, defensive distillation and preprocessing method. Among these methods, there are optimal defense methods for different attacks.Based on the previous manifold analysis and defense methods analysis, we propose an orchestration defense strategy, Manifold Distance Judge (MDJ), which is combining the manifold and the image preprocessing method.

This paper makes four contributions. First, through the analysis of the manifold hypothesis, we propose the manifold distance detection method to reduce the pass rate of adversarial samples by expanding the manifold distance between legitimate and adversarial samples. Second, simple image transformation can enhance the difference in feature distribution between adversarial samples and legitimate samples. Third, we propose a well-designed orchestration strategy, MDJ, for detecting adversarial examples. MDJ combines the manifold distance detection method and input image preprocessing method to obtain a high detection rate of adversarial samples. Meanwhile, the MDJ method we propose can be used as a supplement to other defense methods. Because the original model does not have to be changed, it is easy to combine with other defenses such as a detection method specifically for a certain type of attack. Moreover, we prove that there is an orchestration strategy which is a simple defense combination method in the image field, and its effect is better than the single simple defense, and the random combination of the weak defense combination method.

## 2 RELATED WORK

In this section, we classify adversarial attack methods and briefly summarize the defense methods of adversarial training, defensive distillation, and preprocessing-based techniques.

## 2.1 ADVERSARIAL SAMPLE GENERATION ALGORITHM

_**Gradient Optimization Model:**_ In a gradient optimization attack model, the attacker knows all the information and parameters inside the model and can generate adversarial samples based on the gradient of a given model to attack the classification model(Mu˜noz-Gonz´alez et al., 2017). We discuss the attacking algorithms used in our experiments, Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM)(Goodfellow et al., 2014), Basic Iterative Method (BIM)(Kurakin et al., 2018), DeepFool(Moosavi-Dezfooli et al., 2016), Acobian Saliency Map Approach (JSMA)(Papernot et al., 2016a), Carlini/Wagner Attacks(Carlini & Wagner, 2017).

_**Transfer Attacks:**_ In the transfer attack model, because the attacker does not know the internal information of the model, it is necessary to query the target model multiple times, use the alternative model and other optimization methods to solve the parameters based on the query results, generate the adversarial sample according to the gradient of the alternative model, and classify model attacks. we discuss the attacking algorithms used in our experiment, Momentum Iterative Fast Gradient Sign Method (MI-FGSM)(Dong et al., 2018),Diverse Input Iterative Fast Gradient Sign Method (DI[2] - FGSM)(Xie et al., 2019).

## 2.2 EXISTING DETECTION METHODS

_**Adversarial Training:**_ The defense method is to superimpose the disturbance that maximizes the loss function in the sample, so it is effective for fixed or large disturbances, even if the attack method with obvious changes in the model gradient is easy to be detected.But adversarial training diminishes

2

the ML model’s accuracy and can make the ML model more exposed to generalization(Carmon et al., 2019). Another disadvantage of Adversarial training based defense techniques is that we need to retrain the model whenever some new attack samples are discovered. It will be hard to update all deployed ML models.

_**Defensive Distillation:**_ The essence of the defense method is to replace the model defense idea, that is, to train a new distillation network based on the original model and its output to classify or predict samples.But distillation techniques work by combining the double model, and the second model uses the first model knowledge to improve accuracy. The black-box attack’s recent improvement makes this out-of-date defense (Chakraborty et al., 2018). The strong transfer-potential of adversarial samples across neural network models (Papernot et al., 2016c) is the main reason for this method’s collapse. It is not robust as simplistic variation in a neural network can make the system exposed to attacks (Carlini & Wagner, 2016). (He et al., 2017) concluded that combining/ensemble weak defenses does not automatically improve a system’s robustness. Also, the ensemble technique remains static and vulnerable to a new attack.

_**Processing-based Techniques:**_ The defense method based on image preprocessing has a defensive effect on migration attacks and gradient attacks. Its essence is to reduce the noise of the sample, to reduce the interference of the adversarial disturbance to the model. For the choice of preprocessing method, we cannot judge which attack is effective, and most of the selection of preprocessing method adopts enumeration method, which is complicated to calculate.

## 3 MANIFOLD HYPOTHESIS AND ADVERSARIAL ATTACK

In this section, we introduce the manifold hypothesis to explain the learning and detection process of the deep learning model, and discuss the effect of this hypothesis in adversarial attacks.

## 3.1 MANIFOLD HYPOTHESIS

The Manifold Hypothesis states that real-world high-dimensional data lie on low-dimensional manifolds embedded within the high-dimensional space(Fefferman et al., 2016). It reflects the local smoothness of the decision function, helps to more accurately characterize the local area, and enables the decision function to better perform data simulation. It also explains why deep learning techniques are able to find useful features and produce accurate predictions from datasets that have a potentially large number of dimensions(Fefferman et al., 2016). The fact that the actual data set of interest actually lives on in a space of low dimension, means that a given deep learning model only needs to learn to focus on a few key features of the dataset to make decisions. Many of the algorithms behind machine learning techniques focus on ways to determine these (embedding) functions.

## 3.2 MANIFOLD HYPOTHESIS APPLIED IN ADVERSARIAL DEFENSE

Figure 1: Manifold learning in defense

3

In the training stage, the models learn the manifold representation of the data and the optimal decision border of classification, taking classification task as an example. The model continuously maps different images on low dimensions to form a manifold and learns by confirming tags. In the model checking stage, the features of the picture are also mapped on low dimensions, and the distance from the manifold determined in the learning process is measured to determine the label output by the model. In the detection stage, the model extracts the characteristics of the input picture and compares it with the stored optimal decision border to determine the classification of the input picture. The optimal decision border affects the classification efficiency of the model. The reason is that too few data points cause the boundary shape to change during smoothing, which makes the appearance of adversarial samples inevitable; on the other hand, due to the excessive pursuit of the accuracy of the boundary, it leads to overfitting, and reduce the generalization and robustness of the model.

From the perspective of binary classification, there is no difference between adversarial samples and correctly classified negative samples. The reason is legitimate samples are extremely sparse in the distribution of high-dimensional space in the real world (take images as an example, MxNx8 black and white images, only the value in the high-dimensional extremely sparse field is meaningful). So we can use low-dimensional manifolds to classify legitimate samples. For deep learning models, if the adversarial samples and legitimate samples usually do not appear in which the samples are discriminated in high-dimensional space, it can be found that they are closer to the manifold than the legitimate negative samples. It leads to the failure to correctly classify legitimate samples into negative samples on the low-dimensional manifold that successfully classifies them.

From the perspective of two attack optimization models, there are two reasons for a classifier misclassifies an adversarial example: (1) The adversarial example is far from the boundary of the manifold of the task. (2) The adversarial example is close to the boundary of the manifold. If the classifier generalizes poorly off the manifold in the vicinity of the adversarial example, then misclassification occurs. The distribution probability of the adversarial sample and the legitimate sample is different, that is, the model decision probability of the adversarial sample and the legitimate sample should be different.

There is a theoretical method that can distinguish between legitimate samples and adversarial samples, that is, using adversarial samples that do not appear in the high-dimensional space of legitimate samples for judgment. This type of attack method against samples is to cross the boundary of the manifold with the smallest distance, and their mapping positions in the low-dimensional space are different from the legitimate samples. We assume that if there is a method that can change the mapping position of the image in the high-dimensional space, and also change the mapping position of the adversarial sample in the low-dimensional space, we can let the model learn the manifold of these samples to compare them. Adversarial samples are distinguished and used as the basis for classifying and detecting samples.

## 3.3 BASIC IDEA OF ADVERSARIAL SAMPLE DETECTION

Regarding how the model classifies samples, the following propositions can prove that there is a detection method that can detect adversarial samples and explain the existing detection methods. We can assume that the manifold is proposition A: then there is manifold M and algorithm A, if x is distributed as P(X), then x is on M. The defense detection uses its negative proposition B: that is, there are manifold M* and algorithm A*. If the distribution of x is not P(X), then x is not on M*.

The classification model is easy to be attacked because the dimensionality of the manifold has been excessively reduced. Increasing the dimensionality of the manifold can distinguish adversarial samples, but it is difficult to solve the direction of increasing dimensionality. Based on the above analysis, we can see that it is feasible to use the manifold analysis deep learning model, and it is also feasible to apply the concept of the manifold to classification detection and to explain the distinction between legitimate samples and adversarial samples.

The focus of using the principle of manifold hypothesis and improving detection performance is how to make low-dimensional manifolds better express high-dimensional data. For each defense method, there should be a corresponding optimal defense attack method, and for each attack, there should be an optimal defense method. We take the analysis of three more common defense methods as examples from the perspective of manifold.

_**Adversarial training:**_ this method can only defend against attacks added in the training phase, the reason is the model extracts the features of the adversarial samples formed by the attack method during training and thus can perform defense.

_**defensive distillation:**_ whether it can build its optimized network is critical to the effectiveness of this defense method. This is determined by whether the optimized network can accurately obtain the optimal discrimination boundary. If the learning effect of the boundary is perfect, the performance of defense against known attacks will be greatly improved. But for unknown attacks, the sample

_**preprocessing methods:**_ preprocessing technologies can defend against different attacks. This is because different preprocessing methods have different effects on the samples, leading to changes in the representation of the picture in the high-dimensional space. For different attack methods, the changes of the attack samples are not the same, and the characteristics of the samples in the low-dimensional space also change, resulting in different attack methods for defense.

Therefore, this paper uses the Heuristics method to learn the original high-dimensional sample space which changes in the low-dimensional manifold. We propose a new idea of service orchestration strategy to combine different methods to expand the manifold distance.

## 4 THE MEANING OF ORCHESTRATION STRATEGY

Regarding the attack method, whether it is an attack based on an optimization model or a migration model, the ultimate goal is to make the label classification of the attack sample different from the original correct classification. From the perspective of manifold analysis, the purpose of the attack method is to find the fastest direction of gradient descent, derive the gradient, so that the shape of the adversarial sample in the high-dimensional space changes. It can also affect the mapping graph of the sample in the low-dimensional space, so that the adversarial sample crosses the boundary of the graph classification, to achieve the goal of a successful attack.

The general idea of the orchestration strategy should be able to change the mapping position of the picture in the high-dimensional space through picture processing and display this change. Now that the defense method can be connected with the attack method through the manifold, how to use this idea becomes the key to the orchestration strategy. Since the probability output of the deep learning model can reflect the distance of manifold distance between different samples, combined with the above analysis of the preprocessing method that has the characteristics of defending against gradient optimization models and transfer attack models, it becomes an ideal orchestration strategy idea. Here, we propose the orchestration strategy Manifold Distance Judge (MDJ) and it uses the output of deep learning and preprocessing methods to expand the distance between manifolds. The MDJ was expressed in the form of combination methods in the experimentand achieved excellent results in the ImageNet dataset(Deng et al., 2009).

## 5 OUR METHODS: MANIFOLD DISTANCE JUDGE

In this section, we introduce the Manifold Distance Judge (MDJ) strategy. It uses the manifold detection method to learn the manifold of samples and uses image preprocessing methods to expand the diversity of manifolds between samples. Manifold Distance Judge is essentially an orchestration defense strategythat achieves the optimal defense effect according to the characteristics of different defense methods.

## 5.1 BASIC IDEA OF OUR METHODS

Manifold distance judge (MDJ) is a detector that can detect whether the input sample is adversarial. The detector uses the combination of manifold distance detection method and the image preprocessing changes to represent the subtle changes of the input samples in the high-dimensional space in the low-dimensional space, achieving the purpose of identifying adversarial samples.

The detector is inspired by manifold hypothesis orchestration strategy and can exist independently as a detection system. This allows our method to be combined with other defenses, promoted, and enhance detection performance. In the selection of detection methods, the focus of improving

performance lies in how to change the input mapping position in the high-dimensional space in deep learning and how to represent the change of position distance.

## 5.2 DETECTOR BASED ON PREPROCESSING

To solve the problem of how to change the mapping position of the picture in the high-dimensional space, we get inspiration from the preprocessing process of the image. The transformation of the image can intuitively change the feature representation of the sample, that is, change the mapping position of the image from a high-dimensional space.

For legitimate samples, this transformation does not change the mapping position much. But for adversarial samples, different transformations change the spatial mapping of the picture a lot, which is verified in the subsequent experimental process. We list the preprocessing methods used as following:

_**Random Zoom:**_ (Guo et al., 2017) considered cropping and rescaling of an image as one of their defenses, which is effectively zooming in on a portion of the image, an approach that was defeated by (Athalye et al., 2018b). We reuse this as one of our defenses, where the distance from each edge of the image is cropped by U[10, 50], independently for each edge.

_**Swirl:**_ We introduce a simple defense which is to apply a weak swirl to the image, rotating the pixels around a randomly selected point in the image. The radius of intensity is randomly selected from U[10, 200], and strength from U[0.1, 2.0]. The angle of rotation, center of rotation, and radius of effect are all randomized(Raff et al., 2019).

_**Median Filter:**_ We use a simple median filter, and follow the same approach as the Guassian blur. The radius of the blur kernel is chosen from r _←_ U[2, 5], with a 50% chance all channels are forced to use the same radius(Raff et al., 2019).

_**JPEG Noise:**_ Using lossy JPEG compression to introduce artifacts was introduced by (Goodfellow et al., 2016). Their work looked at how different values of the JPEG compression level (a range from 1 to 100) reduced the impact of adversarial attacks for different values of _ϵ ≤_ 16. However, it was subsequently defeated, having 0% effectiveness (Athalye et al., 2018a). When using this approach, we randomize it by selecting the compression level from U[55, 95].

## 5.2.1 DETECTOR BASED ON PROBABILITY DIVERGENCE

To indicate the change of position distancewe propose a manifold distance detection method, which utilize the output probability of softmax layer of DNN model. The probability can measure the distance of the sample to the decision border. Most neural network classifiers implement the softmax function at the last layer.

The output of softmax is a probability mass function over the classes. The input to softmax is a vector L called logit. Let rank(L,i)be the index of the element that is ranked the i _[th]_ largest among all the elements in L. given a normal example whose logit is L, the goal of the attacker is to perturb the example to get a new logit L2 such that rank(L,1) _̸_ = rank(L2,1)

Use f(x) to represent the output of the last layer (softmax) of the neural network and x as the input of the neural network. For the categories that have been determined, the neural network has a corresponding output probability for each category. We believe that these probabilities represent the comparison between the model’s features for the input x and the model’s learned classification features. The size of the probability output represents the shape of the input x in the low-dimensional manifold mapping and the distance from this type of feature in the low-dimensional manifold mapping.

We use a simple method,

to reflect the transformation of the distance of the picture manifold. Using only a single probability means that the effect is not significant for adversarial attacks, especially for adversarial samples that

## 6 EXPERIMENT

In this section, we explain the experiment results for single method and the combination method after orchestration, and the comparison with Feature Squeezing.

Table 1 and Table 2 shows the detection rates for successful adversarial examples for each attack method. It can be obtained from TABLE 1 that the mainfold detection method performs better under black-box and gray-box attacks, and the ability to reach a detection model above 0.65 thresholds is weak. In contrast, in the white-box scenario, the attack algorithm generated a powerful ability to counter the sample interference model, which also led to the failure of the mainfold detection method in some attack methods in TABLE2. For instance, the detection rate of BIM attacks is only 0.01. The reason is that BIM, as a variant of FGSM, has efficacious interference to the model, which makes it impossible to distinguish samples under the current threshold. Second, in a CW attack, the attack only changes a limited number of pixels. Moreover, in CW attack, it only changes a limited number of pixels, so the Next class as the targeted attack has a greater impact on the detection model, resulting in an increase in the detection rate of the least-likely class as the targeted attack. This is consistent with the observation result of the mainfold detection method in TABLE2, that is, the mainfold detection method can improve the accuracy of model detection to varying degrees.

The detection effect of the preprocessing method is the opposite of that of the math method, and it has a higher detection rate in the white-box scene. As the attack algorithm learns more about the model, the more the features of the images can be changed by the preprocessing method, which in turn affects the judgment of the model. The observations in TABLE1 and TABLE2 confirm our conclusion. Secondly, for different sample preprocessing methods, in white-box attacks, the detection rates of JPEG Noise and Random Zoom are significantly higher than those of Swirl. The reason is that these two processing methods can blur or enlarge the image as an integrated, and change the features more than the Swirl method. However, the JPEG Noise method has no significant effect on FGSM and BIM attacks. In the gray-box scenario, the Swirl preprocessing method has a superior detection rate under MI-FGSM attacks. It illustrates that in different scenarios, each attack method has the most effective preprocessing way for it, and every preprocessing method can change the manifold characteristics of the image to a certain extent.

- 6.2 RESULT-PERFORMANCE OF THE COMBINATION METHOD

TABLE3 and TABLE4 show the detection result of using the orchestration detective strategy to successfully attack samples. Comb1, Comb2, and Comb3 respectively represent the three kinds of Manifold Distance Judge’s orchestration strategies.

_**Comb1**_ Comb1 is only to set up the threshold after performing the same image input transformation listed on the top of the table and use manifold detection method to make judgments. _**Comb2**_ Comb2 combines two single defense methods simply. In Comb2, we first performed image transformation on the samples and set up the threshold of manifold detection. After completing the image transformer, we checked the labels of the images to find out the examples which labels have changed before and after the transformation, as well as the examples whose output of softmax is higher than the threshold for filtering, to achieve the effect of classifying adversarial samples. _**Comb3**_ Comb3 firstly sets up a threshold, and perform manifold detection method on input samples. We filter samples above the threshold, perform image transformation on samples below the threshold, judge the change of the sample label, and use manifold detection method to detect again.

For these three orchestration strategies, Comb1 is equivalent to a random combination method. Compared to Comb2, Comb3 not only has an extra level of detection process but also the two-level detection method uses the idea of orchestration.

The first column in TABLE3 and TABLE4 indicates different preprocessing methods. We can see that for each preprocessing method, the detection rates of Comb1, Comb2, and Comb3 are rising significantly. Meanwhile, in a white-box attack scenario, a reasonably orchestrated Comb3 defense strategy, such as the Random Zoom method used in the preprocessing stage, can achieve a detection rate of more than 95% for all attacks. In the black-box attack scenario, the Comb3 combined defense strategy uses methods, such as Random Zoom and Median Filter in the preprocessing stage, to achieve a detection rate of 85% for all attacks. It further confirms our conclusion that the Manifold Distance Judge method designed by applying orchestration can effectively improve the accuracy of the model.

In summary, Manifold Distance Judge is meaningful, because after the preprocessing transformation, the distance between the legitimate sample and the adversarial sample on the manifold changes differently. In the orchestration defense strategy, we compared the performance of Comb1, Comb2, and Comb3, and found that an orchestration strategy that carefully detects changes in feature distances, such as Comb3, can obtain the best detection effect.

## 6.3 THE COMPARISON

We compare our results with Feature Squeezing(Xu et al., 2017) in Table 5. Comb represent the Com3 in the previous Section4. We configured the Feature Squeezing detectors on ImageNet following the same experiment set-up d in Feature Squeezing paper and reported the detection performance with our target models and the detection dataset. It shows that our recall value on the same white box attack is significantly higher than feature squeezing, especially on CW attacks, we can detect all pairs of samples, and the best classification rate of feature squeezing is only 0.87.
The experimental effect proves that our orchestration defense strategy is better than other combined defense methods, which further verifies the contribution point 3.

## 7 CONCLUSION

From TABLE1 to TABLE4, we can evaluate the classification efficiency of the orchestrated combined defense model. The experimental results illustrated that the performance of a single detection method was inferior or equivalent to that of an ensemble combination of detection methods. However, the performance of the orchestration detection method was far better than that, which shows that the defense performance of this method has been improved significantly. Meanwhile, there were different combinations in the orchestration detection methods. The different input transformation methods will affect the final detection effect, and the choice of math method will also affect the final detection effect. From their perspective, we optimized the arrangement. The input transformation chose the Median Filter, and the math method selected the difference method instead of the maximum value, which highlights the significance of the orchestration.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes 'Manifold Distance Judge' (MDJ), an orchestration-based defense strategy that combines image preprocessing with a manifold-distance-based detection mechanism to identify adversarial samples. While the empirical results on ImageNet show improved detection rates compared to baseline methods like Feature Squeezing, the paper lacks a rigorous theoretical foundation for its manifold distance metrics and fails to account for adaptive white-box attacks that target the orchestration logic itself.

The paper introduces a practical, modular defense strategy that integrates multiple preprocessing techniques via service orchestration. However, the reliance on heuristic distance metrics and the lack of a robust evaluation against adaptive attackers significantly limit its current contribution. The manuscript needs more formal grounding for its 'manifold distance' claims and a more rigorous stress-testing of its detection logic.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Theoretical Justification for Manifold Distance Metrics: In Section 5.2.1, the authors define the manifold distance using a simple difference between the top-two softmax probabilities (dx = p(max) - p(secondmax)). This metric lacks a formal connection to the Manifold Hypothesis described in Section 3.1. There is no proof or empirical evidence provided that this specific logit difference correlates with the geometric distance of a sample from the underlying data manifold. Without such a link, the detector is merely a heuristic ensemble of preprocessing steps rather than a principled manifold-based defense. The authors should provide a theoretical derivation or a visualization (e.g., t-SNE) showing that adversarial samples indeed occupy a distinct region in this probability-difference space compared to legitimate samples.

- Vulnerability to Adaptive White-Box Attacks: The evaluation in Section 6 focuses on standard attack methods but ignores adaptive attacks that specifically target the MDJ orchestration logic. If an attacker has knowledge of the service orchestration (the specific sequence of preprocessing and thresholding), they can optimize their perturbation to minimize the manifold distance after the preprocessing steps, effectively bypassing the detector. The paper assumes the detector remains effective even when the attacker knows the defense, but provides no evidence that the defense is non-differentiable or otherwise robust to gradient-based optimization of the entire pipeline. Future revisions must include an evaluation against an adaptive adversary who has full access to the MDJ logic and can compute gradients through the preprocessing functions.

- Ambiguity in 'Service Orchestration' Mechanism: The term 'service orchestration' is used throughout the paper, yet Section 5 lacks a clear definition of how this orchestration is implemented or optimized. It is unclear if the orchestration strategy (e.g., Comb3) is learned, fixed, or dynamically selected based on input characteristics. If the strategy is fixed, the 'orchestration' claim is misleading; if it is dynamic, the paper fails to explain the selection criteria or the computational overhead involved. The authors need to clarify the architecture of the orchestration layer and provide a latency analysis to demonstrate that this approach is viable for real-world deployment.

- Gradient Masking and False Sense of Security: The defense relies on non-differentiable or stochastic preprocessing (e.g., random zoom, swirl, JPEG noise) to detect adversarial samples. This is a classic recipe for gradient masking (as noted in Athalye et al., 2018), where the defense appears effective against white-box attacks only because the attacker cannot easily compute gradients through the preprocessing operations. The paper does not address whether the observed robustness is due to genuine detection of adversarial features or simply the obfuscation of the model's decision boundary. The authors must demonstrate that their defense holds up against Expectation over Transformation (EOT) attacks, which are designed to circumvent such stochastic defenses.

- Absence of Computational Overhead Analysis for Orchestration: The paper positions the MDJ as an orchestration strategy, yet it fails to quantify the latency impact of the multi-stage detection process (Comb3). Since Comb3 involves multiple passes of image preprocessing and potentially multiple forward passes through the target model, the computational cost could be prohibitive for real-time applications. A complete evaluation must include a latency analysis comparing the inference time of the baseline model versus the MDJ-protected model. This should report the average processing time per image on a standard GPU to demonstrate that the defense is practically viable.

- Lack of Sensitivity Analysis for Detection Thresholds: The detection logic relies on a threshold for the manifold distance metric (dx), but the paper does not systematically analyze how the choice of this threshold affects the trade-off between True Positive Rate (TPR) and False Positive Rate (FPR). Without a Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve or an analysis of the threshold sensitivity, it is impossible to determine if the performance gains are robust or merely artifacts of a specific, manually tuned threshold. The authors should provide a plot showing the detection rate versus the false alarm rate across a range of threshold values to prove that the MDJ is not overly sensitive to parameter selection.

- Need for Statistical Significance Across Random Seeds: The reported detection rates in Tables 3 and 4 are presented as point estimates without confidence intervals or variance metrics. Given that the preprocessing methods (Random Zoom, Swirl, etc.) are stochastic, the detection performance may fluctuate significantly depending on the random seed. To establish that the MDJ strategy consistently outperforms single defenses, the authors must report the mean and standard deviation of the detection rates calculated over multiple independent trials. This is essential to confirm that the observed improvements are statistically significant and not due to favorable random initialization.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a rigorous evaluation against adaptive attacks that optimize directly against the full MDJ pipeline, including EOT-based white-box attacks.

- Replace the heuristic probability-difference metric with a more formal definition of manifold distance or provide empirical validation that this metric captures manifold structure.

- Clarify the 'service orchestration' architecture and include an analysis of the computational overhead and latency introduced by the multi-stage detection process.

- Discuss the potential for gradient masking and provide evidence that the defense is not merely obscuring the model's gradient for the attacker.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding white-box robustness
comments.quote: Tests on the ImageNet dataset, the MDJ is effective against the most adversarial samples under whitebox, graybox, and blackbox attack scenarios.
comments.explanation: The claim that the defense is effective against white-box attacks is problematic because the method relies on image preprocessing. Such defenses are notoriously susceptible to gradient-based optimization when the attacker has full access to the pipeline. Without demonstrating that the defense is robust to adaptive attacks—specifically those that compute gradients through the preprocessing steps—the assertion of white-box effectiveness is likely an artifact of gradient masking. Please qualify this claim or provide evidence that the defense remains effective when the attacker can differentiate through the orchestration logic.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance reporting for Swirl/BIM in Table 4
comments.explanation: The claim that 'the detection rates of Comb1, Comb2, and Comb3 are rising significantly' is contradicted by the data in Table 4 for the Swirl/BIM attack scenario. The detection rates for Comb1, Comb2, and Comb3 are 0.12, 0.08, and 0.15 respectively. The performance of Comb2 is lower than Comb1, and the improvement from Comb1 to Comb3 is marginal. Please reconcile the text's general claim of consistent improvement with these specific results where the orchestration strategy fails to provide meaningful detection.

comments.title: Misclassification of Transfer Attack as Query-Based
comments.quote: Transfer Attacks: In the transfer attack model, because the attacker does not know the internal information of the model, it is necessary to query the target model multiple times, use the alternative model and other optimization methods to solve the parameters based on the query results
comments.explanation: The definition provided conflates transfer attacks with black-box query-based attacks. A transfer attack exploits the property that adversarial examples generated on a surrogate model often remain adversarial for a target model, requiring zero queries to the target. The description of 'querying the target model multiple times' describes a score-based or decision-based black-box attack. Rewrite the definition to state that transfer attacks rely on the transferability of adversarial perturbations from a surrogate model to the target model without requiring interaction with the target.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in the contrapositive of the manifold proposition
comments.quote: We can assume that the manifold is proposition A: then there is manifold M and algorithm A, if x is distributed as P(X), then x is on M. The defense detection uses its negative proposition B: that is, there are manifold M* and algorithm A*. If the distribution of x is not P(X), then x is not on M*.
comments.explanation: The stated 'negative proposition B' is neither the logical negation nor the contrapositive of the original statement. By using this construction to justify detection, the authors implicitly assume a biconditional relationship without providing evidence that the manifold M is uniquely defined by the distribution P(X). This leap ignores the possibility of samples existing off the manifold that still follow the distribution, or samples on the manifold that do not follow the distribution. Rewrite the definition of the detection logic to explicitly state the assumption of a biconditional relationship or derive the detection criteria from the contrapositive.

comments.title: Inconsistent classification of defense methods
comments.quote: On the other hand, in systematic adversarial sample detection methods, there are also methods such as adversarial training, defensive distillation and preprocessing method.
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly categorizes adversarial training and defensive distillation as 'systematic adversarial sample detection methods.' These are standard defense strategies designed to increase model robustness or mask gradients, not detection methods intended to identify and reject adversarial inputs. Labeling them as detection methods contradicts the earlier paragraph in the same section where they are correctly identified as approaches to hardening the classifier. Please revise this sentence to distinguish between robust training techniques and actual detection mechanisms.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a preprocessing-based detection pipeline called Manifold Distance Judge (MDJ) that combines randomized image transformations with a softmax confidence gap to identify adversarial examples. While the orchestration idea of chaining preprocessing and detection stages is intuitive, the manuscript suffers from a fundamental mismatch between its theoretical framing and actual implementation, incomplete evaluation metrics, and missing practical deployment analysis. Addressing these gaps is necessary before the contribution can be fairly assessed.

The paper tackles a relevant problem in adversarial defense by exploring how simple preprocessing steps can be combined with detection heuristics. The empirical tables demonstrate that the proposed Comb3 pipeline improves detection rates over single-stage methods on several white-box and black-box attacks. However, the theoretical justification relies on a misapplied manifold hypothesis, the evaluation lacks essential metrics for defense systems, and the comparison to modern baselines is insufficient.

Overview issues:

- Conflation of Softmax Confidence Gap with Geometric Manifold Distance: The manuscript repeatedly invokes the manifold hypothesis to justify its detection method, yet the proposed manifold distance metric is simply the difference between the top-1 and top-2 softmax probabilities ($dx = p(\text{max}) - p(\text{secondmax})$), as stated in Section 5.2.1. This quantity measures classifier confidence, not the geometric distance of a sample to a learned data manifold. The theoretical link between softmax output gaps and actual manifold proximity is never established, nor is any manifold estimation procedure implemented. Without a formal definition or empirical validation that this probability gap correlates with true manifold distance, the core theoretical framing remains unsupported. Readers will question whether the method actually exploits manifold geometry or merely leverages a known classifier artifact. The authors should either replace this heuristic with a genuine manifold distance estimator or reframe the contribution around confidence-based detection, explicitly acknowledging the lack of geometric guarantees.

- Incomplete Evaluation Metrics and Undefined Detection Thresholds: The experimental section reports detection rates exclusively on successfully attacked samples, omitting clean accuracy, false positive rates on benign data, and the specific thresholds used for the $dx$ metric. A defense strategy that achieves high recall on adversarial examples but degrades clean performance or generates excessive false alarms is practically unusable. Furthermore, the paper never specifies how detection thresholds are selected or whether they require dataset- or model-specific recalibration. This omission obscures the real-world applicability of the method, especially since preprocessing steps like JPEG compression and random zooming can shift probability distributions unpredictably. The authors must report the full detection curve alongside clean accuracy, disclose the exact threshold values, and demonstrate threshold stability across different models and datasets.

- Outdated Baselines and Absence of On-Manifold Attack Evaluation: The experimental comparison in Section 6.3 is limited to Feature Squeezing and does not include modern preprocessing-based defenses or contemporary detection methods such as Mahalanobis distance, ODIN, or randomized smoothing variants. This makes it difficult to assess whether the proposed orchestration strategy offers a genuine advance. More critically, the paper does not evaluate performance against on-manifold adversarial attacks, which recent literature identifies as a primary weakness for distance-based defenses. Since on-manifold perturbations preserve semantic content and often maintain high classifier confidence, a method relying on probability gaps or simple geometric heuristics is likely to fail. The authors should benchmark against at least three recent preprocessing or detection baselines and include a dedicated evaluation on on-manifold attacks to validate the robustness claims.

- Missing Computational Overhead and Deployment Feasibility Analysis: The title and introduction frame the method as a service orchestration strategy intended for real-time deployment, yet the paper provides no analysis of inference latency, memory footprint, or throughput overhead. Each preprocessing step adds computational cost, and the two-stage Comb3 pipeline compounds this expense. Without quantifying the additional inference time relative to a standard forward pass, the claim of practical deployability remains unsubstantiated. The authors should measure and report end-to-end latency per image, compare it against baseline models and Feature Squeezing, and discuss whether the overhead is acceptable for production environments. If the latency is prohibitive, the paper must explicitly acknowledge this limitation and target use cases where offline or batch processing is feasible.

- Unsubstantiated Claim of Theoretical Proof for Orchestration Strategy: The introduction asserts that the authors prove the superiority of an orchestration strategy, yet the manuscript contains no formal proof or mathematical derivation. The claim is supported solely by empirical tables showing higher detection rates for Comb2 and Comb3 over Comb1. In machine learning defense literature, empirical superiority does not constitute a proof, especially when the underlying mechanisms are highly stochastic and model-dependent. The authors should remove the word prove and replace it with empirically demonstrate, or provide a rigorous analysis showing why the specific combination rules mathematically reduce false negatives under bounded perturbation assumptions. This adjustment is necessary to align the paper's claims with the evidence provided.

- Missing Feature-Space Validation of Manifold Expansion: The manuscript repeatedly frames its detection mechanism in terms of manifold geometry, yet it never measures the actual distance between clean and adversarial samples in the model's latent space. Relying solely on the softmax confidence gap leaves the core theoretical premise unverified. Readers will question whether the method genuinely exploits low-dimensional data structure or merely leverages a known classifier artifact. To ground this claim, the authors should compute and visualize the true feature-space trajectories. I recommend adding a t-SNE projection of penultimate-layer activations for clean, adversarial, and preprocessed samples, alongside a table reporting the average cosine distance to the clean data centroid. This direct measurement would confirm whether the proposed preprocessing actually expands the geometric separation the paper promises.

- Undefined Threshold Selection and Calibration Protocol: The orchestration strategy depends on fixed thresholds for the probability difference metric, but the paper provides no guidance on how to select or calibrate these values. This gap compromises the stated goal of creating a model-agnostic supplement, as threshold sensitivity typically forces dataset-specific tuning. Without a clear calibration procedure, practitioners cannot deploy the detector without extensive hyperparameter search. The authors need to explicitly detail the threshold selection process. I suggest including a sensitivity analysis that plots detection rate and false positive rate against varying threshold values, and reporting the exact cutoffs used in the main tables. If the thresholds vary significantly across models, the paper must acknowledge this limitation and propose a standardized calibration protocol.

- Absence of Adaptive Attack Evaluation: The experimental section evaluates MDJ against standard gradient-based and transfer attacks that assume a static defense pipeline. This evaluation design ignores the fact that modern adversaries routinely incorporate known preprocessing steps into their optimization routines. Testing only against unaware attackers leaves a critical blind spot regarding the method's practical robustness. If an attacker knows the MDJ preprocessing is applied, they can easily craft perturbations that survive JPEG compression or median filtering. I recommend benchmarking the defense against adaptive attacks like AutoAttack or PGD variants where the preprocessing operations are approximated or unrolled during gradient computation. Demonstrating resilience under these conditions is necessary to substantiate the paper's broad robustness claims.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Replace the softmax confidence gap with a formally defined manifold distance estimator or reframe the contribution around confidence-based detection, explicitly acknowledging the lack of geometric guarantees.

- Report clean accuracy, false positive rates, and explicit threshold selection procedures, including stability analysis across different models and datasets.

- Benchmark against at least three modern preprocessing or detection baselines and evaluate performance on on-manifold adversarial attacks.

- Provide latency, memory, and throughput measurements for the orchestration pipeline to substantiate claims of real-time deployability.

comments.title: Critical Typo in Softmax Definition
comments.quote: $\text{softmax}(l)_{i}\,=\,\frac{\exp(l_{i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}\exp(l_{i})}$
comments.explanation: The denominator sums $\exp(l_i)$ over $j$, which yields $n \exp(l_i)$. This cancels with the numerator to produce a constant $1/n$ for every class, failing to normalize logits into a valid probability distribution. The index in the exponential term inside the summation must match the summation index. Rewrite the denominator as $\sum_{j=1}^{n}\exp(l_{j})$ to correctly normalize the logits.

comments.title: Scope Mismatch: Graph vs. Image Focus
comments.quote: Attackers can deceive the DNNs model by manipulating graph structure or node features, which results in graph counter disturbances, and limits their application in safety-critical systems.
comments.explanation: The introduction abruptly shifts to graph neural networks by mentioning graph structure and node features. The rest of the manuscript focuses exclusively on image classification and image preprocessing. This conflation misleads readers about the paper's scope. Remove this sentence entirely, as the manuscript never addresses graph data or GNN-specific attacks.

comments.title: Preprocessing Mischaracterized as Training
comments.quote: training a classifier to distinguish between normal and adversarial examples*(Fischer et al., 2017)*, e.g., Preprocessing.
comments.explanation: The third category claims to involve training a classifier to distinguish between normal and adversarial examples, but the cited example is Preprocessing. Input preprocessing alters the input distribution before it reaches the model. Training a detector is a distinct paradigm. Rewrite this to accurately describe preprocessing as modifying inputs to suppress perturbations before classification, rather than training a separate detection model.

comments.title: Conflation of Transfer and Query Attacks
comments.quote: In the transfer attack model, because the attacker does not know the internal information of the model, it is necessary to query the target model multiple times, use the alternative model and other optimization methods to solve the parameters based on the query results, generate the adversarial sample according to the gradient of the alternative model, and classify model attacks.
comments.explanation: This passage conflates transfer attacks with query-based attacks and contains an internal contradiction. Standard transfer attacks craft perturbations on a surrogate model and apply them to a target model without querying the target. Conversely, query-based attacks rely on repeatedly querying the target model for predictions. The text claims both querying the target model multiple times and using gradients from an alternative model, which are mutually exclusive strategies. Clarify which paradigm is actually employed and correct the definition to match standard adversarial machine learning terminology.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Defensive Distillation
comments.quote: But distillation techniques work by combining the double model, and the second model uses the first model knowledge to improve accuracy. The black-box attack's recent improvement makes this out-of-date defense (Chakraborty et al., 2018).
comments.explanation: Defensive distillation was introduced specifically to smooth the model's output and obscure gradient information for defense, not to 'improve accuracy.' Furthermore, Chakraborty et al. (2018) demonstrated that defensive distillation is highly vulnerable to white-box attacks (specifically the C&W attack), not primarily black-box attacks. The description conflates standard knowledge distillation with defensive distillation and misattributes the failure mode. Rewrite to correctly state that defensive distillation aims to smooth the decision boundary and was shown to be highly vulnerable to white-box attacks.

comments.title: Incorrect Attribution and Conflation in Manifold Hypothesis
comments.quote: The Manifold Hypothesis states that real-world high-dimensional data lie on low-dimensional manifolds embedded within the high-dimensional space(Fefferman et al., 2016). It reflects the local smoothness of the decision function, helps to more accurately characterize the local area, and enables the decision function to better perform data simulation.
comments.explanation: The Manifold Hypothesis is a widely recognized intuition in machine learning regarding data distribution geometry, not a formal result established by Fefferman et al. (2016). That paper addresses the algorithmic problem of fitting manifolds to noisy data, not the hypothesis itself. Attributing the statement of the hypothesis to this citation is incorrect. Furthermore, the hypothesis describes where data points concentrate in high-dimensional space; it does not inherently reflect or guarantee the smoothness of the decision function. Smoothness of the classifier is a separate assumption. Rewrite the opening sentences to correctly attribute the hypothesis to standard ML literature and explicitly separate the data geometry assumption from the decision boundary smoothness assumption.

comments.title: False Equivalence of Adversarial and Benign Samples
comments.quote: From the perspective of binary classification, there is no difference between adversarial samples and correctly classified negative samples.
comments.explanation: This claim misrepresents the adversarial threat model. Adversarial samples are specifically perturbed to exploit model vulnerabilities, often resulting in misclassification or confidence manipulation, whereas correctly classified negative samples are benign out-of-distribution inputs. Treating them as functionally identical ignores the structural differences in how they interact with the decision boundary. The authors should explicitly distinguish between benign negatives and adversarial perturbations, clarifying that any shared property must be formally defined rather than assumed.

comments.title: Incoherent Sparsity Claim
comments.quote: reason is legitimate samples are extremely sparse in the distribution of high-dimensional space in the real world (take images as an example, MxNx8 black and white images, only the value in the high-dimensional extremely sparse field is meaningful).
comments.explanation: The parenthetical explanation is mathematically incoherent. An MxN grayscale image with 8-bit depth forms a dense vector in R^(MxN); there is no 'high-dimensional extremely sparse field' inherent to this representation. The claim that only values in a sparse field are meaningful contradicts standard image encoding and offers no basis for invoking the manifold hypothesis. Replace this with a correct statement about the curse of dimensionality, explaining how natural images concentrate on a low-dimensional submanifold within the full pixel space, and remove the reference to a 'sparse field.'

comments.title: Logical Fallacy: Inverse Mistaken for Negation
comments.quote: We can assume that the manifold is proposition A: then there is manifold M and algorithm A, if x is distributed as P(X), then x is on M. The defense detection uses its negative proposition B: that is, there are manifold M* and algorithm A*. If the distribution of x is not P(X), then x is not on M*.
comments.explanation: Proposition B is the inverse of Proposition A, not its negation. In formal logic, the negation of 'If P then Q' is 'P and not Q', while the inverse 'If not P then not Q' is not logically equivalent and cannot be derived from the original statement. Relying on the inverse as a detection criterion introduces a fundamental logical gap. Furthermore, treating a geometric object (manifold) as a logical proposition is a category error. The detection principle should be framed as a heuristic assumption rather than a formal logical deduction.

comments.title: Reversal of Manifold Hypothesis Premise
comments.quote: The focus of using the principle of manifold hypothesis and improving detection performance is how to make low-dimensional manifolds better express high-dimensional data.
comments.explanation: This statement inverts the standard manifold hypothesis. The hypothesis posits that high-dimensional data naturally lies on or near a low-dimensional manifold, not that low-dimensional manifolds should be forced to express high-dimensional data. The goal of manifold learning is to discover or learn a low-dimensional embedding that preserves the intrinsic geometric structure of the high-dimensional data. The current phrasing suggests an unnatural dimensional expansion that contradicts established theory and lacks mathematical grounding. Rewrite to align with the formal definition of the manifold hypothesis.

comments.title: Incoherent Geometric Terminology
comments.quote: It uses the manifold detection method to learn the manifold of samples and uses image preprocessing methods to expand the diversity of manifolds between samples.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'expand the diversity of manifolds between samples' is mathematically incoherent within the context of manifold learning and differential geometry. Data samples are assumed to lie on a single underlying low-dimensional manifold (or a union of class-specific manifolds), not multiple manifolds situated 'between' individual samples. Furthermore, 'diversity' is not a defined geometric property that can be expanded in this context. Preprocessing techniques typically perturb samples, potentially pushing them off the clean data manifold or altering their local neighborhood structure, but they do not generate new manifolds between points. Reformulate the claim to describe the actual geometric effect of the preprocessing step on sample distribution or distance metrics.

comments.title: Missing Units for Crop Parameters
comments.quote: We reuse this as one of our defenses, where the distance from each edge of the image is cropped by U[10, 50], independently for each edge.
comments.explanation: The uniform distribution U[10, 50] lacks explicit units, creating a reproducibility failure. If the values represent pixels, the operation is undefined for standard benchmark datasets like CIFAR-10 (32x32) or MNIST (28x28), where cropping 10 to 50 pixels from each side would remove the entire image. If percentages are intended, the notation should be U[0.10, 0.50] or explicitly labeled as such. Furthermore, cropping independently from each edge without specifying how the resulting non-square or undersized regions are handled leaves the pipeline mathematically ill-defined. Clarify units and post-processing steps.

comments.title: Table 1 Threshold Claim Contradiction
comments.quote: Table 1 and Table 2 shows the detection rates for successful adversarial examples for each attack method. It can be obtained from TABLE 1 that the mainfold detection method performs better under black-box and gray-box attacks, and the ability to reach a detection model above 0.65 thresholds is weak.
comments.explanation: The quoted claim directly contradicts the numerical data presented in Table 1. The Math column for the mainfold detection method reports values of 0.91, 0.86, 0.74, and 0.65 across the listed black-box and gray-box attacks. All of these values meet or exceed the 0.65 threshold, making the statement that the ability to reach this threshold is 'weak' factually incorrect. Inaccurate data interpretation compromises the credibility of the performance evaluation. Rewrite to accurately reflect that the method consistently exceeds the 0.65 threshold.

comments.title: Table 1 Swirl Claim Contradiction
comments.quote: In the gray-box scenario, the Swirl preprocessing method has a superior detection rate under MI-FGSM attacks.
comments.explanation: This statement is inconsistent with the numerical results in Table 1. For the gray-box MI-FGSM attack, the Swirl column reports a detection rate of 0.30, which is the lowest among all listed methods (Math: 0.74, JPEG Noise: 0.67, Random Zoom: 0.74, Median Filter: 0.74). Claiming Swirl is 'superior' misrepresents the table and contradicts the authors' own earlier observation that JPEG and Zoom are significantly higher than Swirl in white-box scenarios. Such contradictions in data reporting create confusion regarding the actual efficacy of the preprocessing steps. Correct the claim to match the tabulated data.

comments.title: Table 2 Targeted Attack Claim Contradiction
comments.quote: resulting in an increase in the detection rate of the least-likely class as the targeted attack.
comments.explanation: The claim that targeted attacks on the least-likely class result in an 'increase in the detection rate' is contradicted by Table 2. For CWL0, the detection rate drops from 0.97 (LL) to 0.35 (Next). For CWL_i, it drops from 0.78 to 0.24. Only CWL2 shows a decrease (0.94 to 0.76), not an increase. The data consistently shows lower detection rates for targeted attacks compared to untargeted ones. Misreading these trends leads to flawed conclusions about how the detector responds to different attack objectives. Rewrite to accurately describe the observed decrease in detection rates.

comments.title: Comb2 Logic Ambiguity
comments.quote: In Comb2, we first performed image transformation on the samples and set up the threshold of manifold detection. After completing the image transformer, we checked the labels of the images to find out the examples which labels have changed before and after the transformation, as well as the examples whose output of softmax is higher than the threshold for filtering, to achieve the effect of classifying adversarial samples.
comments.explanation: The logical structure of Comb2's filtering rule is ambiguous and potentially contradictory. The text states it filters 'examples whose output of softmax is higher than the threshold,' but a higher softmax confidence typically indicates a clean sample, not an adversarial one. Furthermore, the conjunction 'as well as' leaves it unclear whether the label-change check and the softmax threshold check are applied conjunctively (AND) or disjunctively (OR). Without a precise boolean rule, the detection logic cannot be reproduced or verified. Rewrite the filtering condition to explicitly specify the logical operator and clarify whether the threshold applies to the softmax confidence or the manifold distance metric.

comments.title: Comb3 Decision Rule Missing
comments.quote: Comb3 Comb3 firstly sets up a threshold, and perform manifold detection method on input samples. We filter samples above the threshold, perform image transformation on samples below the threshold, judge the change of the sample label, and use manifold detection method to detect again.
comments.explanation: The two-stage filtering logic in Comb3 lacks a clear termination condition for adversarial samples. The text states that samples 'above the threshold' are filtered out initially, and those 'below the threshold' undergo transformation and a second detection pass. However, it does not specify how adversarial samples are identified after the second pass. Are samples flagged as adversarial if they fail the second manifold detection, if their labels change, or if they exceed the threshold again? The absence of a defined decision rule for the second stage makes the orchestration strategy incomplete. Add a precise statement defining the final classification rule after the second transformation and detection step.

comments.title: Numerical Inaccuracy in Performance Claim
comments.quote: Meanwhile, in a white-box attack scenario, a reasonably orchestrated Comb3 defense strategy, such as the Random Zoom method used in the preprocessing stage, can achieve a detection rate of more than  $95\%$  for all attacks.
comments.explanation: While the claim specifies Random Zoom, the phrasing 'for all attacks' implies universal robustness within that category, which is contradicted by Table 4. For the Random Zoom + Comb3 configuration, the detection rate for BIM and FGSM attacks is exactly 0.95, not strictly greater than 0.95. Stating 'more than 95%' is mathematically inaccurate given the reported values. Remove 'more than' and replace with 'at least 95%' or specify the exact range observed to align with the tabulated results.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a defense strategy called Manifold Distance Judge (MDJ) that combines manifold distance detection with image preprocessing to detect adversarial examples. While the idea of using an orchestration strategy to combine multiple weak defenses is interesting, the paper suffers from several critical issues including lack of rigorous mathematical formalization, insufficient experimental validation, and unsupported theoretical claims.

The paper attempts to address an important problem in adversarial machine learning by proposing a detection-based defense that does not modify the target model. The orchestration concept of combining manifold distance metrics with preprocessing methods has practical appeal. However, the paper's theoretical grounding is weak, the experimental evaluation lacks rigor (no confidence intervals, limited attack diversity, no ablation studies), and the claimed contributions are not clearly demonstrated. The paper reads more like a preliminary workshop submission than a mature conference paper.

Overview issues:

- Lack of rigorous mathematical formalization of the manifold distance metric: The paper claims to use 'manifold distance' as a detection criterion but never formally defines what this distance is. Section 5.2.1 introduces a simple heuristic dx = p(max) - p(second max) as a proxy for manifold distance, but provides no justification that this quantity actually measures distance on or off the data manifold. The paper conflates softmax output differences with geometric manifold distances without any theoretical connection. This is a fundamental issue because the entire detection method hinges on this purported manifold distance. The authors need to either formally define a proper manifold distance (e.g., geodesic distance on the learned manifold) or clearly state that they are using softmax output differences as an approximation, with empirical validation that this approximation works.

- Insufficient experimental validation and missing standard metrics: The experiments report only detection rates (recall) without false positive rates, ROC curves, or AUC metrics. This is a critical omission for a detection method, as a detector that flags everything as adversarial would achieve 100% detection rate but be useless. The paper also does not report confidence intervals or standard deviations, making it impossible to assess the statistical significance of the results. The evaluation uses only a limited set of attacks (FGSM, BIM, DeepFool, C&W, MI-FGSM, DI-FGSM) and does not include modern strong attacks like AutoAttack or PGD with random restarts. Without proper ROC analysis and evaluation against adaptive attacks, the claimed detection performance cannot be trusted.

- No ablation studies to isolate component contributions: The paper proposes MDJ as a combination of manifold distance detection and image preprocessing, but never runs ablation studies to determine how much each component contributes to the final performance. The three combination strategies (Comb1, Comb2, Comb3) compare different orchestration rules but do not isolate the effect of the manifold distance detection itself versus the preprocessing methods. A proper ablation would include: (1) manifold detection alone, (2) each preprocessing method alone, (3) manifold detection + each preprocessing method, (4) random combinations as baselines. Without these, it is unclear whether the orchestration strategy adds value beyond simply using the best single preprocessing method.

- Unsupported claim about orchestration strategy superiority: The paper claims that 'there is an orchestration strategy which is a simple defense combination method in the image field, and its effect is better than the single simple defense, and the random combination of the weak defense combination method' (Section 1). However, this claim is not properly tested. The comparison with Feature Squeezing (Table 5) is the only external baseline, and Feature Squeezing is a single method, not a combination. The paper does not compare against other ensemble or combination defense methods. Moreover, the claim that Comb3 is 'carefully orchestrated' while Comb1 is 'random combination' is misleading — Comb3 simply adds an extra detection step, which could trivially improve detection at the cost of increased false positives (which are not reported).

- Theoretical analysis is vague and non-rigorous: Sections 3.2 and 3.3 attempt to use manifold hypothesis to explain adversarial attacks and defenses, but the reasoning is imprecise and contains logical leaps. For example, the paper states 'we can prove that in the deep learning model, the accuracy and generalization of the model cannot be taken into account at the same time' (Section 1) — this is presented as a proof but no actual proof is given. The discussion of 'proposition A' and 'negative proposition B' in Section 3.3 is mathematically incoherent. The paper would benefit from either providing a proper theoretical framework (e.g., defining manifolds, distances, and detection criteria formally) or dropping the pretense of theoretical contribution and focusing on empirical findings.

- Missing comparison with state-of-the-art detection methods: The only baseline comparison is with Feature Squeezing (Xu et al., 2017), which is a relatively old method. The paper does not compare against more recent detection-based defenses such as MagNet (Meng & Chen, 2017), Local Intrinsic Dimensionality (LID), Mahalanobis distance-based detection, or other manifold-based detectors like those by Jha et al. (2018) or Bhagoji et al. (2020). Given that the paper claims to propose a 'well-designed orchestrated defense strategy,' it must demonstrate superiority over a range of existing detection methods on standardized benchmarks. The absence of such comparisons makes it impossible to assess the relative contribution of MDJ.

- Unclear experimental setup and reproducibility concerns: The paper states experiments are on ImageNet but does not specify which subset, how many images, the target model architecture, or the attack parameters (epsilon values, number of iterations, etc.). The description of the three combination strategies (Comb1, Comb2, Comb3) is vague — for example, 'set up the threshold' without specifying how the threshold is chosen. The paper also does not mention whether the attacks are successful (i.e., only adversarial examples that fool the classifier are used for detection evaluation) or how many samples are used. Without these details, the experiments cannot be reproduced or verified. The paper should follow standard evaluation protocols (e.g., those used in the RobustML benchmark) and provide code or detailed pseudocode.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a rigorous mathematical definition of the manifold distance metric used for detection, or clearly state that softmax output differences are used as a proxy and validate this choice empirically.

- Add comprehensive experimental evaluation including ROC curves, AUC metrics, false positive rates, and confidence intervals. Include evaluation against strong adaptive attacks like AutoAttack and PGD with random restarts.

- Conduct ablation studies isolating the contribution of each component (manifold detection alone, each preprocessing method alone, and all combinations) to justify the orchestration strategy.

- Compare against a broader set of state-of-the-art detection methods (MagNet, LID, Mahalanobis detection, etc.) on standardized benchmarks with proper experimental setup documentation.

- Clarify the experimental setup: specify ImageNet subset size, target model architecture, attack parameters, threshold selection method, and provide code or detailed pseudocode for reproducibility.

comments.title: Contradiction between ensemble claim and paper's own method
comments.quote: (He et al., 2017) concluded that combining/ensemble weak defenses does not automatically improve a system's robustness. Also, the ensemble technique remains static and vulnerable to a new attack.
comments.explanation: The paper cites He et al. (2017) to argue that combining weak defenses does not automatically improve robustness, yet the core contribution of this paper (MDJ) is precisely an orchestration strategy that combines multiple weak defenses (manifold distance detection + image preprocessing). This creates a direct contradiction: the paper uses a citation to argue against its own approach without explaining why MDJ's orchestration is different from the 'naive ensemble' criticized by He et al. The paper must either reconcile this contradiction by explaining how MDJ's orchestration overcomes the limitations identified by He et al., or remove the citation if it undermines the paper's main claim.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about proving accuracy-generalization tradeoff
comments.quote: In the analysis of defense methods using the manifold hypothesis, we can prove that in the deep learning model, the accuracy and generalization of the model cannot be taken into account at the same time. That is, when we improve the performance of one aspect, we will sacrifice another one.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to 'prove' a tradeoff between accuracy and generalization using the manifold hypothesis, but no proof is provided in this section or elsewhere in the paper. This is a strong theoretical claim that would require rigorous mathematical derivation (e.g., a formal theorem with assumptions and proof steps). The manifold hypothesis alone does not imply such a tradeoff. The authors should either provide the proof or rephrase this as a conjecture or observation rather than a proven result. Rewrite as: 'Based on the manifold hypothesis, we conjecture that in deep learning models, accuracy and generalization may be in tension, motivating the need for an adversarial defense system.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about orchestration strategy superiority
comments.quote: Moreover, we prove that there is an orchestration strategy which is a simple defense combination method in the image field, and its effect is better than the single simple defense, and the random combination of the weak defense combination method.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to 'prove' that an orchestration strategy outperforms single defenses and random combinations, but no proof is given in this section or elsewhere. The experimental section (Section 6) compares only three specific combinations (Comb1, Comb2, Comb3) against Feature Squeezing, not against all possible random combinations or a formal proof. The claim is not supported by the paper's content. The authors should either provide a formal proof or rephrase as an empirical finding. Rewrite as: 'Our experiments demonstrate that the proposed orchestration strategy can outperform individual simple defenses and random combinations in certain settings.'

comments.title: Missing false positive rates and ROC metrics
comments.quote: TABLE3 and TABLE4 show the detection result of using the orchestration detective strategy to successfully attack samples. Comb1, Comb2, and Comb3 respectively represent the three kinds of Manifold Distance Judge's orchestration strategies.
